name: Apolaki
description: Apolaki, also known as Apolaqui, is a major deity in pre-colonial Philippine mythology, particularly among the Tagalog and Kapampangan people. He is primarily recognized as the god of the sun, war, and warriors. Often depicted as a strong and valiant figure, Apolaki embodies courage, strength, and unwavering determination. Some myths portray him as the son of Anagolay, the goddess of lost things, and Dumagan, a powerful warrior. He is often in conflict with his sister, Mayari, the goddess of the moon, leading to the cycle of day and night. Apolaki's influence extends to navigation and seafaring, as the sun's position is crucial for sailors. He is revered for his ability to bring light and warmth to the world, dispelling darkness and providing sustenance. Warriors often invoke his name for strength and victory in battle. He is a symbol of power and represents the indomitable spirit of the Filipino people. His legends are passed down through generations, reminding people of the importance of bravery, perseverance, and the constant struggle between light and darkness. He is also associated with healing, particularly through the sun's life-giving energy. Apolaki's legacy continues to inspire and shape cultural beliefs in the Philippines.
subtitle: God of Sun, War, and Warriors

keyMyths.title: The Conflict with Mayari
keyMyths.description: Apolaki and Mayari constantly fight, resulting in the cycle of day and night. Apolaki's strength represents the sun's dominance during the day.

keyMyths.title: Apolaki's Lineage
keyMyths.description: Apolaki is often described as the son of Anagolay and Dumagan. This lineage connects him to both loss and strength.

keyMyths.title: Apolaki as a Guide
keyMyths.description: Sailors used Apolaki's position (the sun) for navigation. This highlights his role in guiding and protecting travelers.
keyMyths.source: Ancient Beliefs and Customs of the Tagalogs
